---
title: "Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies"
subtitle: "Unit 5 Task 3"
author: "David Martinez"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import pandas as pd 
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

from IPython.display import display

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

<!-- Rather than a simple write up like we have been doing so far, this project requires a more structured approach. You will need to show you have completed the tasks, but will structure the report in a way that is more like a client report. The report will be structured as follows:

 - Executive Summary: This is a high level overview of the analysis, including the problem statement, the data used, and the main findings. It should be concise and to the point, summarizing the key insights/recommendations without going into too much detail. These are usually bullet points, no graphs. PLEASE EXPLICITLY STATE IN THIS SECTION WHERE YOU DID THE STRETCH OR NOT! 
 - Methods: this is a high level overview of the methods used in the analysis. This should include an explanation of data preparation steps, especially how you dealt with each categorical variable and why. Showing the resulting or original distribution of variables may be helpful. Describe how you handled missing values and outliers. How did/might your decisions impact results?Explain any feature engineering steps you took. This is also where you will put the graphs you recreated to show you validated the dataset and that it matches the article.
 - Results: this is a more in-depth explanation of results. A classification report and feature importance graphs belong here.
 - Conclusion: this is where you will discuss the implication of the results, including any limitations of the analysis, and recommendations for actions/future work. It may feel repetitive to the executive summary, but it should be more detailed and include any additional insights you gained from the analysis. -->

## Executive Summary

The main goal of this project is to clean and prepare the data so we can latter use it to build a machine learning model to predict whether a person makes at least $50K or more based on ther demographics and other movie related data.

The data was originally collected by FiveThirtyEight torught a survey, the survey was focus on Star Wars movies including questions about which movies they have seen, which one is their favorite, and some demographic questions.

The data was cleaned and validated by recreating two of the visuals from the original article. I used two models (Random Forest and Gradient Boosting) to predict whether a person makes at least $50K or more, the best model achieved an accuracy of around 65%.

I did complete the stretch goals for this project, including recreating a third visual and building a more precise machine learning model.


## Methods

Data Preparation: The data was first prepared by renaming the columns for ease of use. Then we filter the data so only people who have watch at least one movie are takeng into account. __This was done in Task 1, Questions 1 and 2__

The data was also "translated" into numbers so the model can use them to predict the target. Age ranges were mapped to the loweest range, education levels got trasnlated to a scale from 1 to 5, income ranges were mapped to the lowest value in the range, and the favorability questions were mapped to a scale from 1 to 5. Other categorical variables were one-hot encoded, this means tranlated to binary variables. The rows that were missing income were dropped, because thee is no way to know if the model predicted correctly or not without the target variable. For other missign values I used the median of the column to fill them. I took the desition to fill the missing values with the median because I waned to keep all the information possible. __This was done in Task 2, Question 1__

Validation: To chek if the data was acurrate I replicated the graphs from the original article to see if the values matched. The data was validated, graphs matched. __This was done in Task 1, Question 3 + Stretch__


## Results

The datas was divided into divided in a 20/80 split for testing and training. "Cheat" colums where dropped because they would give the asnwer away to the model. 
Two modelst were used:
Random Forest: I have used this model regularly trought the semester and it has deliver good results, but it wasnt the best in this case, it got an acceptable 63% acurracy.

Gradient Boosting: I tried this model for the Stretch and it gave good results, I had to tweak some parameters like the estimators (50), and the max depth (3). This model got an acurracy of 65%.

My conclution is that the data seems so indicate that there is a correlation btween demographics, Star Wars movies, and income. There was definetely a pattern that the two models found and used to predict income succesfully.


__TASK 1__  
__QUESTION 1__  
__Shorten the column names and clean them up for easier use with pandas.__
Provide a table or list that exemplifies how you fixed the names.

```{python}
df = pd.read_csv("https://github.com/fivethirtyeight/data/raw/master/star-wars-survey/StarWars.csv", encoding_errors='ignore',skiprows=1)

# Assigning clean column names
new_columns = [
    'RespondentID', 'Seen_Any', 'Fan', 
    'Seen_Ep_I', 'Seen_Ep_II', 'Seen_Ep_III', 'Seen_Ep_IV', 'Seen_Ep_V', 'Seen_Ep_VI',
    'Rank_Ep_I', 'Rank_Ep_II', 'Rank_Ep_III', 'Rank_Ep_IV', 'Rank_Ep_V', 'Rank_Ep_VI',
    'fav_Han', 'fav_Luke', 'fav_Leia', 'fav_Anakin', 'fav_ObiWan', 'fav_Palpatine', 
    'fav_Vader', 'fav_Lando', 'fav_BobaFett', 'fav_C3PO', 'fav_R2D2', 'fav_JarJar', 
    'fav_Padme', 'fav_Yoda', 'Shot_First', 'Familiar_EU', 'Fan_EU', 'Fan_StarTrek', 
    'Gender', 'Age', 'Household_Income', 'Education', 'Location'
]

df.columns = new_columns

example_fixes = pd.DataFrame({
    'Original Question / Header': [
        'Have you seen any of the 6 films in the Star Wars franchise?', 
        'Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace', 
        'Han Solo'
    ], 
    'New Column Name': ['Seen_Any', 'Seen_Ep_I', 'fav_Han']
})

display(example_fixes)

``` 

__QUESTION 2__  
__Filter the dataset to 835 respondents that have seen at least one film__
(Hint: Don’t use the column Have you seen any of the 6 films in the Star Wars franchise?)

```{python}
seen_cols = ['Seen_Ep_I', 'Seen_Ep_II', 'Seen_Ep_III', 'Seen_Ep_IV', 'Seen_Ep_V', 'Seen_Ep_VI']
df_seen = df.dropna(subset=seen_cols, how='all').copy()

print(f"Total respondents who saw at least one film: {len(df_seen)}")

```


__QUESTION 3 + STRETCH__  
__Validate that the data provided on GitHub lines up with the article by recreating 2 of the visuals from the article.__
These visuals should be similar, including titles, subtitles, gridlines, order of categories, colors, etc. But, they don’t have to be exact. They need to be close enough that we can validate that the values in the dataset match the graphs in the chart. Though their charts were built using a different plotting software, the more you push yourself for an exact replica, the more you will learn. Spend at least a couple of hours on this.

```{python}
# Visual 1: Which movies have you seen?
seen_pct = df_seen[seen_cols].notnull().sum() / len(df_seen) * 100
movie_names = ['The Phantom Menace', 'Attack of the Clones', 'Revenge of the Sith', 'A New Hope', 'The Empire Strikes Back', 'Return of the Jedi']

data1 = pd.DataFrame({'Movie': movie_names, 'Percentage': seen_pct.values})
data1['Movie'] = pd.Categorical(data1['Movie'], categories=movie_names[::-1], ordered=True)

chart1 = (
    ggplot(data1, aes(x='Movie', y='Percentage')) +
    geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.6) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(title="Which 'Star Wars' Movies Have You Seen?", subtitle="Of 835 respondents who have seen any film", x="", y="")
)
display(chart1)

# Visual 2: Best Star Wars Movie
df_all6 = df_seen.dropna(subset=seen_cols, how='any').copy()
rank_cols = ['Rank_Ep_I', 'Rank_Ep_II', 'Rank_Ep_III', 'Rank_Ep_IV', 'Rank_Ep_V', 'Rank_Ep_VI']
best_pct = (df_all6[rank_cols].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce') == 1).sum() / len(df_all6) * 100

data2 = pd.DataFrame({'Movie': movie_names, 'Percentage': best_pct.values})
data2['Movie'] = pd.Categorical(data2['Movie'], categories=movie_names[::-1], ordered=True)

chart2 = (
    ggplot(data2, aes(x='Movie', y='Percentage')) +
    geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.6) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(title="What's the Best 'Star Wars' Movie?", subtitle=f"Of {len(df_all6)} respondents who have seen all six films", x="", y="")
)
display(chart2)

# Visual 3 (Stretch): Who Shot First?
shot_data = df_seen['Shot_First'].dropna().value_counts(normalize=True) * 100
data3 = pd.DataFrame({'Answer': shot_data.index, 'Percentage': shot_data.values})
answer_order = ["Han", "Greedo", "I don't understand this question"]
data3['Answer'] = pd.Categorical(data3['Answer'], categories=answer_order[::-1], ordered=True)

chart3 = (
    ggplot(data3, aes(x='Answer', y='Percentage')) +
    geom_bar(stat='identity', fill='#008fd5', width=0.2) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(title="Who Shot First?", subtitle="According to 834 respondents", x="", y="")
)
display(chart3)
```

__TASK 2__  
__QUESTION 1__  
__Clean and format the data so that it can be used in a machine learning model__. As you format the data, you should complete each item listed below. In your final report provide an excerpt of the reformatted data with a short description of the changes made.

```{python}
age_map = {
    '18-29': 18,
    '30-44': 30,
    '45-60': 45,
    '> 60': 60
}

edu_map = {
    'Less than high school degree': 1,
    'High school degree': 2,
    'Some college or Associate degree': 3,
    'Bachelor degree': 4,
    'Graduate degree': 5
}

income_map = {
    '$0 - $24,999': 0,
    '$25,000 - $49,999': 25000,
    '$50,000 - $99,999': 50000,
    '$100,000 - $149,999': 100000,
    '$150,000+': 150000
}

fav_map = {
    'Very favorably': 5,
    'Somewhat favorably': 4,
    'Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)': 3,
    'Somewhat unfavorably': 2,
    'Very unfavorably': 1,
    "I don't understand this question": np.nan, 
    'Unfamiliar (N/A)': np.nan
}

df_seen['age_numeric'] = df_seen['Age'].map(age_map)
df_seen['edu_numeric'] = df_seen['Education'].map(edu_map)
df_seen['income_numeric'] = df_seen['Household_Income'].map(income_map)

df_seen['target_50k'] = np.where(df_seen['Household_Income'].isna(), np.nan, (df_seen['income_numeric'] >= 50000).astype(int))

fav_cols = [col for col in df_seen.columns if 'fav_' in col]
for col in fav_cols:
    df_seen[col] = df_seen[col].map(fav_map)

df_seen = df_seen.drop(columns=['Age', 'Education', 'Household_Income'])

categorical_cols = df_seen.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'category']).columns
df_encoded = pd.get_dummies(df_seen, columns=categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
```


__TASK 3__

```{python}
ml_data = df_encoded.dropna(subset=['target_50k']).copy()

cheat_columns = [
    'target_50k', 
    'RespondentID', 
    'income_numeric',
    'Household_Income'
]

X = ml_data.drop(columns=cheat_columns, errors='ignore')
y = ml_data['target_50k']

X = X.fillna(X.median())

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=37)

rf_model = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=37)
rf_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = rf_model.predict(X_test)
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)

print(f"Overall Model Accuracy: {accuracy:.2%}\n")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))
```


__STREACH__  
__QUESTION 1 + QUESTION 3__  
__Build a machine learning model that predicts whether a person makes at least $50k with accuracy of at least 65%. Describe your model and report the accuracy.__

```{python}
categorical_cols_final = df_seen.select_dtypes(include=['object', 'category']).columns
df_final = pd.get_dummies(df_seen, columns=categorical_cols_final, drop_first=True)

ml_data_final = df_final.dropna(subset=['target_50k']).copy()

cheat_columns = [
    'target_50k',
    'RespondentID',
    'income_numeric',
    'Household_Income'
]

X_final = ml_data_final.drop(columns=cheat_columns, errors='ignore')
y_final = ml_data_final['target_50k']

X_final = X_final.fillna(X_final.median())

X_train_f, X_test_f, y_train_f, y_test_f = train_test_split(X_final, y_final, test_size=0.2, random_state=37)

gb_model2 = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=50, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=3, random_state=37)
gb_model2.fit(X_train_f, y_train_f)

# 6. Predict and Evaluate
gb_preds = gb_model2.predict(X_test_f)
gb_acc = accuracy_score(y_test_f, gb_preds)

print(f"Simple Gradient Boosting Model Accuracy: {gb_acc:.2%}\n")
print("--- CLASSIFICATION REPORT ---")
print(classification_report(y_test_f, gb_preds))
```
